title: Nail Polish on Wool
timeEstimate: Pro-only — do not improvise at home
stainChemistry: Nail polish is polish film + plasticizers + pigment + ester solvents (sometimes acetone). On wool (wool protein), the cotton + silk lessons compound: acetone bleeds wool dyes AND can damage keratin. "Non-acetone" remover has uncontrolled ester concentrations that strip wool dye. Isopropyl alcohol bleeds wool dyes. Water flooding + wool = felting risk. Wool's banned-tools list (no enzymes, no hot water, no alkaline, no agitation) eliminates almost everything. Pro uses amyl acetate (controlled ester) at strict dilution + cold NSD + acetic acid neutralization, all without flooding the wool. Home cannot match this safely.
whyThisWorks: A wool specialist with cold amyl acetate + tight blotting + cold NSD can lift fresh polish without felting or stripping wool dye. Home cannot — every household solvent damages wool, and water-flood + wool = felt risk. Honest home protocol: lift bulk if wet, leave alone if dry, pro within 24 hours.
defaultAssumption: Wool + nail polish = pro IMMEDIATELY. Home applies NO solvent. Best outcome reaches the cleaner with nothing applied.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Amyl acetate, white cloth, hidden seam
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply amyl acetate to a hidden seam for 30 sec. Check for color bleed, felting, or texture change. Any of those = abort, refer to wool damage protocol.

spottingProtocol.dwellTime: 5–10 minutes total, refresh cloth each pass
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply to film with white cloth, tight blotting concentric passes, refresh cloth each pass. Do NOT flood wool — water + agitation = felt.
Sponge gently with minimum moisture, press only. Removes residual pigment without felting wool.
Dropper for pH restoration. STRICT 60-sec dwell. Press dry, lay flat, no heat.

steps: 1. Hidden-seam amyl acetate test — abort on bleed / felt / texture change.
2. Amyl acetate tight-tamp blotting, concentric passes.
3. Cold NSD sponge with minimum moisture.
5. Press dry, lay flat to air-dry.
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient solvent + cold rinse — no heat
warnings: NEVER acetone on wool — bleeds dye + damages keratin
NEVER nail polish remover at home — uncontrolled esters
NEVER isopropyl alcohol on wool — bleeds wool dyes
NEVER agitation — felts wool on contact when wet

homeSolutions.instruction: Nail polish on wool is the rare combination where every home solvent causes new damage. Acetone bleeds wool dyes and can damage keratin. "Non-acetone" removers have uncontrolled ester concentrations that strip wool color. Isopropyl alcohol bleeds wool dyes. Water flooding + wool = felting risk. The cotton playbook does not transfer. Best move: leave the stain alone, take to a wool specialist within 24 hours.

homeSolutions.agent: If Wet — Press Once Gently, Then Stop
homeSolutions.instruction: If the polish is still liquid, press a dry white cloth STRAIGHT DOWN on the pool, outside-in. Lift straight up — no wiping, no smearing. 2–3 light dabs. Do NOT add water — water on wool + agitation = felt. Surface bulk only.

homeSolutions.agent: If Dry — Leave It Completely Alone
homeSolutions.instruction: Dried polish on wool is EASIER for a pro to handle than disturbed polish. Do NOT pick at it with a fingernail. Do NOT scrape — scraping shreds wool fibers. Do NOT attempt to soften with anything. Leave it in place.

Not every dry cleaner handles wool. Call ahead: "Do you treat wool with amyl acetate for nail polish?" If they don't understand, try another cleaner. Tell them: "Nail polish on wool, [wet still / dried]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no remover, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing."

steps: 1. Wool + nail polish = pro IMMEDIATELY. NO home solvents.
2. If wet: 2–3 light dabs straight down with white cloth. Stop.
3. If dry: leave alone. Do not pick, scrape, or soften.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Always stop after the initial blot. Any home solvent on wool creates new damage worse than the polish.
warnings: NEVER acetone on wool — bleeds dye + damages keratin
NEVER nail polish remover (any kind) on wool
NEVER alcohol / IPA on wool — bleeds wool dyes
Hidden-seam test belongs to the pro

neverDo: Never nail polish remover (acetone or non-acetone) at home
fiberSensitivities: Wool dyes bleed from acetone, IPA, and uncontrolled ester solvents
Wool felts on contact under heat + moisture + agitation
Amyl acetate is the only known wool-safe solvent — pro-only

materialWarnings: Pro IMMEDIATELY — no safe home solvent for nail polish on wool
Cotton + silk + nail-polish playbooks all damage wool
Don't pick or scrape dried polish — shreds wool
Don't flood wool with water — felt risk

escalation.when: Always — wool + nail polish is pro-only IMMEDIATELY. The cleaner's best outcome is the one where you bring it in with NOTHING applied.
escalation.whatToTell: "Nail polish on wool, [wet still / dried], age [hours / days]. NOTHING applied at home. No acetone, no remover, no alcohol, no water, no scrubbing."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner specializing in wool

contextQuestions: Wool type — merino / regular / felt / blend?
Has anyone tried anything yet (acetone, alcohol, remover)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Pre-applied acetone or remover = wool dye likely already bled where applied. Pre-applied IPA = wool likely partially dye-stripped. Pre-applied water + rubbing = wool may already be partially felted. Disclose honestly — changes pro plan. Merino felts faster; felt fabric is pre-felted (harder for pigment removal but no further felt risk).
